topic: Traits - Implementing Iterator Adapters as Structs
content: The reason why we wrap iterators in structs instead of just calling methods on the iterator itself is that it solves a fundamental problem with the Iterator trait: state mutability. When you look at the Iterator trait definition, you will notice it only exposes methods like next() and has_next(), which are purely functional; they take no arguments and return zero mutable references to external data. This creates an immediate mental block because if your adapter needs to maintain a window size for sliding windows or track a running sum for accumulators, the pure functional interface simply cannot express that requirement without breaking the abstraction. The solution we use in Rust is to implement our own trait, often called AdapterIterator or similar, which extends Iterator and adds a state field of type T where T holds all the mutable information our adapter needs to maintain across iterations. This approach allows us to chain these adapters together because each layer can hold its own internal state while delegating the core iteration logic to an inner iterator that might be another adapter or a primitive collection like Vec or String. When you implement this pattern, you are not just writing code; you are building a composable architecture where every adapter acts as a transparent wrapper around the next level of abstraction. The magic happens in how we handle the yield expression inside the Iterator trait implementation for our struct; we do not simply forward yield to the inner iterator but instead perform state transitions before yielding each item, ensuring that the internal counters or buffers are updated exactly when the consumer requests data. This design choice is critical because it means that if you chain multiple adapters together, say a filter followed by a take_n adapter, the second adapter can inspect and modify its own internal state based on the items produced by the first, creating a pipeline where information flows directionally from producer to consumer while each stage maintains independent context. In practice, this pattern is so powerful that we frequently use it to implement complex algorithms like LZW compression or moving average calculations without needing to write explicit loops in our application code; the iterator itself becomes the loop, and the struct holds the algorithmic logic as its internal state machine. You will often encounter this pattern when working with higher-order functions because they typically return an Iterator type rather than consuming it immediately, which means we must provide a concrete implementation that can be cloned or moved into our application context to actually perform the iteration. The key insight is that by treating iterators as first-class structs with state, we gain the ability to reason about side effects and resource management within the iterator itself, avoiding the need for manual loop tracking in our calling code and giving us a clean, declarative way to build complex data transformations.
